commentBody:
Think I'll get a TEMPORARYTATTOO of some [ossicones] for the next time in OUTINLA and there are TENANTS in my FLAT. ILLSTART the LATESTFAD. See you at the RAWBAR. WORDS.
Thank you, Hannah.
Whew! Nice workout. It gave me that thrill that I might not get it. Thank you. Great construction and clues and everything.
Easy but good fun. And this came out in The Onion just yesterday so absolutely perfect timing for 7D- https://theonion.com/rita-oras-agent-scores-singer-another-prime-crossword-puzzle-placement/

What a delight! The northern half was as tough and unrelenting as any I've done for ages. Very difficult to start a wedge anywhere. The south fell into place much better, and I surprisingly bested my Friday average. Not that I cared. Just finishing a gem like this is its own satisfaction.
Good mix of challenging and easy. Too many names though.

This is the Saturday I’ve been waiting for.  Tough all around, but especially the NW corner.  I thought for a minute that I might have to call a crossword HOTLINE, but eventually I eked out the solve.  What a delight.

Tough puzzle today, I don't always manage to finish Saturdays.  Especially only after looking things up when I've already correctly guessed or filled them to confirm correctness.

I got the right side of the puzzle relatively quick for a Saturday but then it took me forever to even get anchors into the left side.

MASHUPS was the last word I got just because I'm so math oriented I couldn't get my mind off something to do with the mathematical concept of composite numbers.  CELESTA crossed with COLOR WARS was a really tough one because I didn't put the former together until I had enough letters to spot the "Celestial" root and didn't think of Color until I had the L.

Thought the BOA clue of "Wraps his meal before eating it" was clever.

Overall about two minutes slower than average, but bearing in mind I finish maybe half of Saturdays that's still on the quick side.

Polonius: What do you enter into this puzzle, my lord?

Hamlet: Words, words, words.  I see here that “Mulan” uses PUNS as antagonists. Also, they do call it a DOOK when one writes GOB ANANAS.  I wonder what is behind that arras? NO PEEKING!

Polonius: Though this be madness, yet there is method in it.

The concrete that is filling my head makes it impossible for me to tell anymore whether a puzzle is easy or hard.

I started off great with this one, filling in 1A without any crosses.  I got the NW corner quickly, then bogged down to a crawl.

Over and over and over I looked at the empty spaces and thought, "This one's got ya, old man".  And then I'd get another couple of letters, and I'd think I'd broken it open and I'm coasting to the end. Then stuck again. Deja vu.

RESHARE was REtweet for a long time, so that was problematic.  And with AUNATURaL, I poisoned myself on the long down entry.

At various times giraffes were sans wings and hands before they didn't have HORNS. 

What in the world are COLORWARS?  Something the US is about to engage in?
28D was likely a smirking reference to us status hungry puzzle solvers.

We couldn't decide what to eat for dessert. It was a banana split decision.

(We'll take it to a court of a-peels.)

Who knows what the mob will say. 
I made zero headway the first pass. 
But I locked onto the RIAN/RAWBAR cross in the SE.  and I was off to the races. 

I think this was Saturday material.  Maybe Saturday-  

I spent too much time trying to figure out my mistake when I finished. Thankfully, finally thought to spell the name GENA instead of GiNA. 
I ain’t ever heard of no CELESTA.  

speaking of which.  I guess that one long time contributor from Connecticut?  who was a pipe organist? Doesn’t post anymore. He was a moody dude.  But generally had interesting things to say. Iirc, he did have some health issues tho.  Hope all is well with him.

Oof! Toughest puzzle I've seen in a while. Lately I get the Fri and Sat with no lookups, but the best I could do here is 2/3 complete before using several Wordplay hints and a few Comments hints.
I hope the "too easy" camp is happy today. Not complaining though - I see lots of people got it no problem so it's a me problem.

Very nice puzzle! Thank you, Hannah! My insecurity led me to once again hit "check puzzle" halfway through. And yet again, all was right. Oh well. I don't keep streaks, but this bad habit of mine deprives me of the deliciously smug smile that accompanies a GOLD STAR. I adore giraffes but just learned about ossicones. I used to play the piano but just learned about CELESTAS. In other words, my solve today was a very fruitful TIL experience in addition to having been delightful. Wrapping it up, here's a shout-out to the Red Hot Chili Peppers with a song I love: https://youtu.be/j9qfClVvfIw?si=QMmAfI-jsTC8EUFC Have a wonderful weekend, everyone, and a glorious month of May!
WORDSWORDSWORDS I can deal with.  Names, names, names not so much.  It all came together in the end, but it felt like a MOONSHOT until it finally took off.
I refuse to believe that this is the first time that DONER has been in the crossword.

I knew CELESTA for one reason and one reason only.

When I was very young, there was a record and a book called "Sparky's Magic Baton".  The baton is magic because it makes musical instruments speak.  You played the record and paged through the books on cue from the record.

For some reason, they had a celesta in the book, and it made the most magical sound.  

I've never seen or heard one since.  Sometimes I almost thought I'd imagined it.

Wow, that's following memory threads almost back to the beginning.
Anyway, only middle schoolers say ANYWAYS.

Well...  Clocked in with a 44 min solve time, several minutes slower than my already plodding Saturday average.  At least I didn't have to use any lookups as I lumbered along channeling my inner rhino.  This was one of those that looked pretty hopeless after the first pass (thought briefly about bailing and calling it a night) but ever so gradually began to take shape.  A bit surprised I got the happy music without having to hunt for mistakes.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
Just what Saturday ought to be.

Precious few toeholds and yet somehow arriving at the summit.

Thank you, setidors.

This was a weird solve for me. I did the NW and SE sections very quickly, then the NE came to me, but the SW had me quite stumped. I switched to pencil mode and started typing in whatever I thought were the most likely answers, and... I was done in 23 minutes with the hole thing, which is faster than my Friday average.

I enjoyed the clueing.

Of course I did not know most of the trivia, but unlike in the SE corner-from-hell of this week's Thursday puzzle, it didn't come bunched up in one area for a perfect storm of naticks, so it ended up not being a huge problem.

Really enjoyed this one. Started with DONER as that was a quick dinner the other night and went from there.  Having not attended summer camp in the US or otherwise I started with TWINRACES, which I thought was very clever until it was not! Had GINA for 28 across until I realised that it had to be GENA given the “heavenly” hint in the down clue.

Was scratching my head at TOE for [Winning X or O] until I wrote it here as a question just now. 
Aha! The penny drops. Nice.

Awesome awesome Saturday. That’s how you do it. Almost no gimmes even on some of the easier entries. I loved that getting any corner still didn’t give you an in on the others so it was push and shove all the way. I had a lot of misdirections—was hoping “lifeless” would be DEAD (what a great double misdirection that would be). I thought some sort of disloyal FAN would be gone tomorrow which led me to guess IRON for the anniversary present. I love YORK peppermint patties (the perfect end for a Skyline chili lunch) but just couldn’t pull it out right away, and since RESHARE was the last to drop I was debating whether giraffes had ossicones instead of CORNS on their feet (ridiculous notion, they have hooves ANYWAYS). Lots of fun clues but my favorite was MASHUPS. Even though I guessed “disappearing ink” would have to do with TATTOOS it wasn’t immediately obvious what that would be. Happy to say I did get ATMINSIDE with no crosses, proud of that one, and CELESTA helped too, but despite all my Shakespeare knowledge WORDS also eluded me at first. Lastly, I too have fond memories of COLORWARS, a summer camp staple (although even there I had COLORDAYS first, it’s certainly been awhile…)

I love the CELESTA. It is perfectly suited to experimental genres, art pop, and post rock. All classical iterations of it are beautiful, from the hauntingly dark timbres of Shostakovich to the soaring, celestial melodies of Holst. However, the brightness of it loves up the Stones’ “She’s a Rainbow” so perfectly, it’s celesta gold.

BOA clue was fab! 

Giraffe baby and mum so sweet! 🧡

As ever, go Gunners! 🏆🏆

Now THAT was a Saturday… thank you. 
I was definitely going nowhere for a good 15 minutes… so satisfying to trawl through it in the end. 
I had ALLOW for the second part of ‘put up with’ n was stuck trying to think of an opposite for DISALLOW… 
Anyway… loved it and a nice way to start the weekend.

Didn't get a TOEhold until RAWBAR/RIAN, but was most relieved when I solved TEMPORARY TATTOO because the only thing I could come up with to that point for 14A was REVEAL ALL in an otherwise mostly empty NW, but that would mean 7D would have to be a different Rita, which would raze the very foundation of everything I thought I knew about NYTimes crosswords. SO ANYWAYS

That was not an easy solve! But it was great puzzle, daunting at first but it slowly began to give way. The last part to fall was the northwest corner, where I fell into trance, didn't think, just let the answers magically fall into place. Strange but true.

As for the northeast I went all over the country city by city trying to match them with their respective NBA teams and the Warriors and Clippers were the only eight letter names I could come up with, but once I got FLAT, and being out already on the west coast, LALAKERS filled in easily. 

Speaking of the Lakers I see they made it to the second round last night, but the highlight of the night was RJ Barrett's game winning three for the Raptors!

Yesterdays constructor (Rena Cohen) made a comment about dabbling in themeless puzzles being a fun challenge. I’d have assumed a straight puzzle would be easier than trying to build around a restrictive theme or trick. Could someone explain that?

Coincidentally, I've been slowly working through the 2025 ACPT Final Puzzle 8-A ("The most difficult version of puzzle 8, solved by the top three finalists in the A division"), in GAMES World of Puzzles magazine, October 2025 issue.

29A: Flipped = WENT BANANAS

49A: Severe proscription, in internet lingo = PERMABAN

I had 29A already, but just got 49A thanks to Hannah's puzzle

Tough Saturday but kept me busy on a sleepless night. Had to look up a couple of names. Bounced around the puzzle a bit. Favorite clues were for temporary tattoos, moonshots, stage name and latest fad. So anyways, have a good day everyone.😊

Excellent Saturday with not much to connect the grid, making it feel like four distinct puzzles.  The NW went so fast I was afraid I would be disappointed, but things slowed down after that.

My solve went NW, SE, NE finishing with the SW and other that the NW, each had its challenges. 

 [Call it!] had me thinking of a referee, so I thought of "in or out".  WEDS kept me from filling that in, but not from trying to come up with another way to say it.  I was hopping around and missed reading the clue for CARESSED.  Once I noticed it, that broke the section open.

Thanks for a fun solve, Hannah.
Whoever comes up with these clues… are you ok
great puzzle!  lots of wordplay.
Are we having “anyways” my fellow solvers? Surely not.
This one came easily to me and, in fact, I finished faster than Friday's which was rated "Very Easy". I guess I was just on the author's wavelength.
A nice, fun puzzle. My favorite was "Disappearing Ink". For 35A my first thought was MOM.

That one answer you finally see, with maybe one or two crosses, that breaks open an entire section. But until that moment, crickets, opaqueness due to vague clues and no-knows.  First, you’re chipping away at a massive stone, and suddenly you hit the magic spot, the stone cracks open, and you’re surfing a wave… until the crickets return.

On easier days, such surfing feels good but doesn’t mean as much.  On Saturday, when it happens, you feel, “Now THAT was good.”  And when It happens several times, you feel, “That was a proper Saturday.”

Creating a puzzle that does this is an art.  Today, for me, the box exuded this artistry.   

Furthermore, every clue felt well thought out.  There was wordplay and humor.  And beauty, especially hard to place in an uber-low 64 word grid:  MASHUPS, MOONSHOTS, NO PEEKING, SO ANYWAYS, ON AND ON, TEMPORARY TATTOO, BAMBINA, and the marvelously clued MASHUPS.  

This was a proper Saturday, Hannah.  I got my Saturday and my soul is happy.  Thank you!
Good stuff
One of those, first time through - I'll never get this. Pick it up, put it down, repeat. But slowly things began to appear. So many no knows. A perfect Saturday, thanks.

This one flew by, 
but what fun!

Felt similar to yesterday’s for me.

Kind of filled itself in
and my only nit is that it was over too quick.


No. My true nit is the word
ANYWAYS.

Typing it just hurt my fingers.

I'm surprised no one has chimed in on the "error" of LA LAKERS and OUT IN LA in the same puzzle.

As we know, the whole entry has to be a dupe to break an unwritten rule, but many people complain about this thing, or about a word being in one clue and a different entry, etc.

Of course, anyone who mentions this later in the day will most certainly not have read the earlier comments first.

Ugh.  I’m afraid my mileage varied on this one!  When I spend half as much time in Wordplay as I do in the grid, I’m not a happy bunny.  (Don’t get me wrong:  I love Wordplay, but usually as dessert, not a main course.)

Still marveling at the clever people who make these puzzles for my enjoying.

Y’all take care and be kind!

Okay, learning about ossicones made my morning 😊

... and the concept of "informal" hand grenades is something I want out of my head. (I know, it's the clue format, but ugh.)
Definitely not in my wheelhouse. I don’t think I knew a single proper noun in the entire puzzle, and there were a lot of them.

This is the way that a challenging Saturday has always gone for me. Almost nothing on the first pass, then the SE falls quickly, The SW follows, then the NE, and invariably, I finish up in the NW. It was gratifying to finally have a puzzle that was challenging, unlike yesterday's. I must have known about DONER from my travels, since I got that immediately, and it's virtually unknown here. 

I had no idea about MOONSHOTS in that capacity, or HUNS, which is what was holding me up in that section. But when I took a chance on that "U" in HUNS, that gave me AU NATUREL, and the rest was history. A good one!

Very nice themeless puzzle, and it seemed more appropriate for its day than yesterday's did. Took me 35 minutes without assistance, which is a bit faster than my Saturday average.

More than a few unknowns (to me) in the grid---ALANA, GENA, COLORWARS, ELISA, DONER, OUT IN LA---but all of these were easily handled with help from crosses.

REMOVABLE rather than TEMPORARY modifying TATTOO slowed me down some.

"One wrapping up a meal before eating anything?" for BOA made me smile, after first considering various spiders.

Upon reviewing the whole puzzle after finishing, I find myself wondering why I found this one as difficult as I did. But I suppose many puzzles look easier in hindsight.

Good hard puzzle. Solved in about 2/3 of my average time. I did a Saturday puzzle from 2015 recently. Took about three times longer than today’s puzzle but I finally got it. There were no crossing nouns that had to be guessed. Just very difficult but solvable clues. I enjoyed both puzzles. Just enjoyed one a lot longer. That made it more satisfying.
The SE corner killed me! Thought I’d get one cleanly today. Oh well.

ANYWAYS! 

Takes me back to middle school and my teachers trying desperately to get us all to cut off the “s” at the end.  If it wasn’t for Ross on Friends later showing us all “the word you are look for is ‘anyway’” we would still be saying it with the S. 

Nowadays, I’m glad my generation leans into the middle school fads. Nothing like making a 12 year cringe watching the adult say “6-7”

Lovely Saturday work-out!  The kind of exercise I like on a Saturday morning.

Normally I'm one to complain about a grid that feels like four Midi's jammed together, but this one didn't fell like that to me, for whatever reasons.

Nothing was coming in the North of the puzzle, and I remembered with tough puzzles it's a good idea to work your way up from the bottom.  But there were few gimmes in the South, as well.
Today was the rarity where I started in the  middle and worked my way outwards.  I began with NANO and CAR, and thanked the Gods that I remembered the celestial CELESTA.

So, it was slow -- fast -- and then slow again, as I tried to puzzle out the top and bottom.

But I got my GOLD STAR and a happy ending.  
Yay, me!
And big thanks to Hannah Slovut-Einertson for an excellent puzzling puzzle.
Joel Fagliano's Mini today was all trivia. I had to look 5 things up to complete it. Has Joel LOST HIS MIND? Concerned for him!
Way too much poorly placed random trivia. It serves zero purpose and ruined what could have been a good puzzle. Misdirects and most other clues were decent.

Ah, the DONER kebab, or Döner, as I was introduced to it.

Thanks to immigrants from Turkey, it's been ubiquitous in Germany for at least 45 years.
And, because it's so delicious, it has spread throughout Europe.

But can one find a decent Döner in the good ol' USA.  I've traveled a good bit in the States and haven't found one yet. If your town or city has a respectable Döner joint, let me know!

And don't tell me about your town's wonderful Gyros.  I love a good gyro; gyros are delicious, but they are the poorer cousins to a well-made Döner.
What's the difference?  Try one, and you won't have to ask.

This was an excellent puzzle! A perfect Saturday difficulty, and full of fun stuff: AU NATUREL, PERMABAN, BAMBINA, CELESTA. It's definitely a puzzle to GO BANANAS over!

Those two giraffes in the picture are too cute. I am going to think about smooching giraffes whenever I need to be cheered up.

Typical tough Saturday for me. More than a couple of complete unknowns (PERMABAN, DONER) and of course a lot of others that were never going to dawn on me without some crosses. No complaints - just made for a nice long workout. 

And... first puzzle find today - a Wednesday from August 3, 2011 by Paul Guttormsson. Every single clue in the puzzle started with a 'T', and... every answer contained at least one T, though many of them had a lot more. Some examples: 

TEMPORARYTATTOO
TENNESSEETITANS
TEETERTOTTERING
HTTP
ATTA 

Amazing work of construction, and there were no answers appearing for the only time. Can't imagine what it would take to work out something like that. 

Might put another puzzle find in a reply. 

....

First, I don't understand the title on the Comments...I get it, but I don't see why it's there.  Distraction?

SO, ANYWAYS (which I entered with a pained expression) ...I was SO disappointed that 40D was not SCYTHE that I left it in there for a ridiculously long time, trying to make it work.  Sigh.  Srsly, Hannah, wasn't this a Missed Opportunity??

Apparently there is no end to the TV shows/actors/film directors/ and athletic teams that are unseen/uncelebrated/unknown to the MOL!  It makes me wonder what on Earth I've been doing with my time...

Hope to check in later --after the Southern Fiber Artists mtg, but before The Kentucky Derby.
Wonderful! I love a puzzle that i can’t finish until after a good night’s sleep - well done!
One of my favorite Saturday puzzles in a while. It had some bite but the excellent grid made it more than worth the effort. As an added bonus, the constructor is from MN. Well done Hannah!
Terrific Saturday work out -- after this, the rest of the day should be a breeze!
Tough puzzle. Really enjoyable solve. Kudos to the author

Giraffes are very social animals.

They just want to belong.

Convenient store sign? I'll give you some.

No Shoes, No Shirt, No Service
No Public Restrooms
No Cash Overnight
No Loitering
No Clerk Access to Safe
Camera on Premise
ATM inside (Pay No Attention To Those Lurkers)

Why are they convenient again? Ah, Keystone Beer and Lotto tickets🤣😛

When I solve a Saturday puzzle and it’s still relatively early in the day I always expect to see comments about the puzzle being too easy. Not so today…so my day is off to a great start! By my standards…an excellent puzzle.
Today's earworm: Let's get down to business to defeat the HUNS.

GOLDSTARS to Hannah, and I got mine, too. Lovely Saturday workout. I didn't know most of the names, and other things, too. Persevered through intuition, and refused to pull up Wikipedia. 
Got my 5 mile walk in before any drizzle started. Time for housework. Have a great day.

I think my DNF today might amuse you.  (I didn't know it was a DNF until I checked the blog). After cheating on DANNY and after very belatedly changing GO BONKERS to GO BANANAS, the SE that was giving me such a headache finally seemed solved.  (It was a headache because two names I didn't know, RIAN and DANNY, were right next to each other and BONKERS made my grid an ungettable mess).  But, Aha!, finally I had it!!!

The film director was gIAN and the "buck-a-shuck" establishment was a GAY BAR.  A place where attractive young bucks shuck their clothes, right?

And this gave me BOY SAW.  Admittedly I've never heard of a BOY SAW, but then I've never heard of a BOW SAW either.  Why a BOY SAW?  I tried to imagine those young bucks in the GAY BAR with their clothes shucked.

Or as Gilda Radner would have said: "Never mind."

Very, very chewy. Far too much white space for far too long had me scratching my head, I had to fight for almost every answer.  As usual, once filled it all looks so straightforward, why did it feel so tricky?
 The hardest to parse was ATM INSIDE. I had TM..S..E for so long and couldn’t make head nor tail of it. I kept thinking opening times.
A real workout Saturday, thank you. Sitting inside listening to the lovely Spring rain greening up the garden. Shame I have to go out and poo pick. That’ll teach me for not doing it this morning.
Another one of those days where I feel like I did a completely different puzzle from everyone else.
Too obscure and difficult for me. Ended my streak of Saturdays.

Way too many proper nouns and obscure trivia.

In other words, awesome Saturday.
Very tough for me, 20 minutes over my average.

I had BE PATIENT crossing WARRIORS in the NE, because I don't follow basketball. But wasn't Steph Curry's team on top for a few years?  And I wondered how "disappearing ink" made something TaMPeR resisTant, but that fit 8D. SO ANYWAYS, I was so far off base they declared me AWOL. 

Finally cleared my mistakes when I got PERMABAN crossing RESHARES. The struggle was real
Probably my fastest Saturday ever? 25 min and did the whole thing in list view. Everything just clicked.

I had PALER as Asian vis-à-vis African elephant, which by crossword standards, is a fact. I guess they are lest plentiful(?)

Now, if I could only distinguish an African swallow from a European swallow. Is it the airspeed velocity? 😛
Solid puzzle, but   ‘heavenly’ in the musical instrument clue and ‘arch’ in the cutting tool cue were Wednesday level giveaways.
I kept wondering what an AUNA TUREL was 🤦🏾‍♂️

I enjoyed this puzzle, but I’m quite surprised to see so many comments describing it as hard. To anyone who considered this hard, or “a proper Saturday”, I’d be curious to hear your thoughts on this one from 2013 that I just encountered in the archives: https://www.nytimes.com/crosswords/game/daily/2013/05/18
if you're performing au naturel, you may want to use a stage name or request "no peeking"

The CELESTA was invented in 1886 by the French reed-organ-builder, Auguste Mustel. Tchaikovsky first saw/heard one five years later, when he was stopping in Paris, on his way to America. Tch. immediately said "Aha! This will wow them in St. Petersburg! But I better get my hands on it, before that sneaky Rimsky-Korsakov, or that hack, Glazunov, beats me to the punch!" So, Tch. arranged for his publisher, Pyotr Jurgenson, to purchase one (for 1200 francs), and have it shipped to Russia, secretly, lest Nikolai or Alexander should find out. Mustel's firm continued in the celesta-business until 1975, when it closed; since then the only two manufacturers of celestas are Yamaha (which makes everything), and the German firm Schiedmayer. Schiedmayer, however, claims that only theirs is a *real* celesta! https://www.classicalcalifornia.org/articles/tchaikovsky-nutcracker
Out in LA is a song. No compilation album was released in 1994. Someone please correct me with citations please.

Tough for me -- surprised to see so many people describe it as easy. Never watched Full House, an author and director I've never heard of, etc. 

But the other clues were awesome and eventually it all clicked. 

Great Saturday!

Haven't read all comments yet. Because I complained about yesterday's lovely-but-wrong-day puzzle, I want to say that I liked this one a lot. It felt like a proper Saturday. My solve time was about 70% of my Saturday average, but it didn't feel easy when I was in the middle of my solve. Several wrong guesses kept me putting things in and ripping them out--deAd before FLAT (10A), rENterS before TENANTS (13D), chAracter before STAGENAME (17A), and MedleyS before MASHUPS (1D). Loved the clues for SWEAT (I got misdirected to running for office), HORNS, BOA, and DOMED. I was sure 8D [Disappearing ink?] was related to the death of print media. Just a class act all around. Thanks, Ms. Slovut-Einertson. As for 49A... If you're local you know about the "Vessel" at Hudson Yards. I once heard it referred to dismissively as the DONER, and now I can't call it anything else. ;) Lastly... if you like epic MASHUPS, check out DJ Earworm. He usually does at least one a year, and if you know your popular music you'll be impressed. Here's an older one, but it's Annie Lennox so I was familiar with a lot of the songs. https://youtu.be/akE0VnS6N88
I love the NYT Crossword because once in a while I am taught something new.  I will never forget my beautiful discovery today.  Thanks to Hannah Slovut-Einertson I learned about The Celesta.

Some say harder some say easier. It’s a Saturday and sometimes it’s hard because there are things you have no clue about. Sometimes it’s easy because everything is in your wheelhouse. I’m forever in awe of the ability to stack multiple words and phrases across and down and they all make (most of the time) sense. This one was pretty much down the middle for me as I finished slightly better than average. Mind exercise over. Nap time.

This was a delightfully difficult Saturday for me. Phew! About halfway through I had holes in every section, but perseverance paid off, and the NW corner finally fell at the end. 
I've never heard of COLORWARS, but the crosses worked so I went with it. And TIL CELESTA, BAMBINA, a fun fact about giraffes, and that DONER is Turkish in origin! Thanks for a fantastic Saturday workout.

So was this one of those cross promotional deals with The Onion? Rita Ora’s Agent Scores Singer Another Prime Crossword Puzzle Placement https://theonion.com/rita-oras-agent-scores-singer-another-prime-crossword-puzzle-placement/
Very nice puzzle. I’d take ‘em even harder than this — this came in just under my Saturday average— but no complaints. Thanks Hannah.
A fun themeless, so many clever clues.  Especially liked BOA — knew it couldn’t be “box.”  Finished under average time for a Saturday once I figured out how to spell CELESTA. Heavenly, indeed

I liked this a lot! Some of it was easy but other things were hard for me. For me, it took me what a good Saturday should take, because of some of the sticking points like I couldn’t think of BAMBINA 
as feminine, so that was a lookup; 4 word checks in the Southwest to get LATEST FAD(LA_EST_AD) , DONER(DO_ER), FRAGS(had eRAGS) and make sure WED was right; and puzzle check for my final word to fall in the Southeast: RAW BAR
(I think we’ve had one of its crosses, BOW SAW before but I need to remember it).

I liked how 32 Down looked like ALLOW
but there were two letters before it and so
SWALLOW contains ALLOW for “Put up with”. 
Had __A__OW.
"Anyways" ??????  I'm aghast.  Otherwise great puzzle.
Excellent Saturday, Hannah!! Your puzzle is a permawin...Grazie!

Now see, this is why I've started checking XWStats before commenting. This one felt very hard to me. I almost gave up several times in the bottom left quadrant and my time was about 40% slower than usual.

But the stats page has this as Average, so it clearly was just outside my wheelhouse. I got the upper right quadrant right away, but struggled in all the others. Maybe because I didn't know the Full House dad's name, the Red Hot Chili Peppers album, or the actress Donovan? 

It may be that all those things were big during a particular decade of my misspent youth when I was largely off the grid. Naturally I've heard of the shows and the band, but the details just never sank in. Maybe due to some of those off-the-grid activities.

I usually allow an hour for Saturdays; this one took 46 minutes. I'm sure that's much slower than many of you out there. I found it quite challenging but was lucky with my guesses on a few of the long ones.

I don't worry about my solving speed.  I don't even mind if it takes me a long time to solve (or if I forget to close the puzzle and it stays open all night).  That makes it easier for me to beat MY average solve time.  Like the Rhino, I just PLOD along.

Remarkable puzzle find. A Sunday from January 18, 1998 by Ed Early and Bob Klahn with the title "Prophecy." The length of the theme answers is quite remarkable, especially since it is a very famous quote from Martin Luther King Jr. I'll put the length at the end of each answer: IHAVEADREAMTHATONEDAYON (23) THEREDHILLSOFGEORGIATHE(23) SONSOFFORMERSSLAVESAND(21) THESONSOF(9) FORMERSLAVEOWNERSWILL(21) BEABLETOSITDOWNTOGETHER(23) ATTHETABLEOFBROTHERHOOD(23) Wow. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=1/18/1998&g=37&d=A Might repost this tomorrow. ....
I enjoyed this puzzle.  Never heard of COLOR WARS, and WOOD seems pretty feeble as a 5th anniversary gift, but the crossings were simple enough.

I hated this with all my heart. There is a point after which puzzles stop being interesting and start becoming irritating. There were multiple adjacent crosses in the lower sections of the puzzle that were completely ungettable unless one knows the highly specific trivia piece.

A puzzle should be able to let you guess some letters of the niche trivia clues based on either the crossing entries or the English language's phonology. Today's puzzle gave neither of these in a few crosses.

Trivia learnt from a puzzle is fun and memorable only if the puzzle didn't suck out the entire life and enthusiasm from the solver.

This was an extremely gratifying solve for me. Mainly because for me, it was very challenging and early on it looked like I would need some lookups. However, one answer at time, I made it to the victory tune. 

Rating:
8/10
Toughie! Good job.

That was a great puzzle!

I enjoyed the many misdirects, some of which were tough for me to parse correctly.

All the ways you could clue the fill "Sam" in today's Mini and they choose Sam Altman. 
If that's the editorial bar, why not give David Berkowitz some extra notoriety?
Pretty fun if knew what the RHCP album was. If you didn't, NW was tough.

@Barry Ancona you seem to be more than a fixture here. Maybe you can tell me. This one stretched me and I got it with no help. It took me 28+ and I feel excellent. I just don’t know how much faster I can get and I think I’m pretty good at these. When I see the times of people who are not using help, for instance, Thursday and Friday this week, I am truly humbled.

How do you guys get them done so much faster than me?

@Jake G

They cheat.

@Francis

Boo!  We do not.  (Actually, I can't speak for everyone, but I'll say from experience that it's possible to solve quickly without cheating.)

And the key is, indeed, experience.  After 40+ years of solving, I know that [Joins a union?] in 4 letters is going to be WEDS.  I know who singer Rita is without looking at the song title, especially if it's three letters.  

You pick up a lot of new vocabulary and trivia with experience and practice, and as a result, you come up with answers faster, and with more certainty.  But also, you should broaden your knowledge by reading on a wide variety of topics, even those you have little interest in.  (I don't listen to Miss ORA myself, although I hear she's pretty good.)

I don't know how long you've been solving, but there was a time when 28 min. on a Sat. would have been satisfactory for me.  Today, I did it in less than a third of that.  (I found it very easy, beating my Sat. average by 30%, despite early stats showing the puzzle to be "Average.")

And I don't go to the tournament because I know there's no way I can come close to winning.  Those guys finish in about two minutes!

@Jake G

I am sure there are many reading this that wonder how you can solve in just 28 minutes. 
It’s a spectrum. 
I think sub 30 is pretty pretty good for a Saturday. 

Coincidentally. I solved in 28:10.  Filled the grid in 24?  Spent the rest of time trying to find my typo/mistake. GiNA got GENA.
@Weak  28:12. Same final square.

Jake,
What Steve said.

There's also the factor that with a lot of practice you learn how to think outside the box.

My times have gradually gotten better during this nine months that I have been subscribing. A dollar/week is tough to pass up. I’ll probably leave when I lose that introductory rate. userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
@Francis

Color War is (was?) a savage rite of upstate New York summer camps in the 60's and 70's (and I can only suppose other locales and eras) wherein the entire camp is divided into two (colored) teams at the end of the summer dividing bunkmates, friends and even counselor couples and for a week or so quotidian life is slavishly and entirely randomly transformed into days and evenings filled with athletic and artistic battles between the two sides. Every skirmish had a winner and a loser with points determining the difference and the ultimate winner of the war was determined by total points accrued at the end of hostilities.

A hatched was buried and often real tears exchanged at war's end and a gaudy plaque was fashioned celebrating that year's winner with the names of principal officers and heroic soldiers and hung on the rafters of the mess hall, like sports banners adorning arenas, for subsequent summers' campers and counselors to see and remember.

This disturbing ritual often devolved into the basest of William-Golding level humiliations but also featured moments of profound personal and team glory.

As with most enterprises involving humans in competition, the former vastly outnumbered the latter.

Having endured a number of these I can safely say these experiences were rather appropriate training for the workplace competitions of adult life.

@Matt

You make me grateful I never went to summer camp.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
@Becky

Always just sounded like a xylophone to me...
@Matt it is basically a xylophone, just with a keyboard mechanism to strike the chime bars instead of a mallet

@Matt

Huh. 

Celestas sound like stars twinkling, bell-like. And xylophones sound like tropical trees, wood-like. LSD aids one to this conclusion. 

Both are idiophones. Xylophones and marimbas are made of wood, with marimbas having a deeper, more mellow and resonant sound. Glockenspiels, celestas, and vibraphones are made of metal. Vibraphones have the deepest and longest vibes, glockenspiels are high-pitched and sharp. All are struck with mallets except the celesta, which as @G Hoper noted, is played via a keyboard similar to a piano.
@G Hoper. To be persnickety that’s a glockenspiel, metal bars, not a xylophone, wooden.

@Charles Peterson

I'll defer to your ear.

If for no other reason than you decided to CHIME in so definitively...(see what I did?)

But NOTHING?

Come on, now.

You will concede, I trust, it sounds way more like a xylophone than, say, an oboe.

@Becky
Nice call-out to the celesta on She’s a Rainbow. That’s Nicky Hopkins on celesta (and piano and harpsichord). A great session keyboardist who contributed his excellence to many bands (including the Kinks, The Who, etc. etc.) and is responsible for many iconic keyboard riffs of the 60s and 70s that people recognize, if not his name. Died in the 90s, I think.

Niall,
I don't think it was about construction difficulty. I read "fun challenge" to mean coming up with exciting clues and answers because there is no exciting theme or trick.

@Niall

The process is different in a few ways.

Simply put-

With a themeless, 
you need fewer black squares, 
a *much* smaller word count,
so those long answers and spanners
need to both stack and sparkle.

Themed puzzle construction
gives you a solid framework,
because the theme is running the show
in terms of grid placement, length,
revealer…

So you get that all done 
and then the job is 
to make the fill not feel like…fill.

So, just different types of challenges.

@Niall

Some people are better at one than the other but for me a good themeless is MUCH harder. Yes it needs a smaller word count for one thing; but more importantly it needs fresh, original fill. For a themed puzzle I can come up with good theme material then allow the software to fill in a lot of the rest; yes it takes a lot of trial and error, understanding grid management and placement, original clueing, all of that, but it can be done. For themeless puzzles you need to find or build around original entries that may not be in the software, manage your own original wordlists, avoid less glue, have a lot of really quality stacks, it’s no joke.

@Niall
Those are interesting answers, thanks. I suppose I’m not considering that the themeless ones are usually later week and sometimes Wednesdays. It’s not really clear to me why there aren’t early week themeless ones with the larger word count and just day-appropriate clueing (day-appropriate being debatable obviously).

@SP

"...allow the software to fill in a lot of the rest..."

I guess it is way past the time for me to accept that software and its evil cousin, AI, are the norm and not a cheat.  But I am a dinosaur that has evolved only so far that I enjoy solving on my PC more than paper and pencil.  But I still hate it when travelling means that I have to do the work on my phone.  Yuck.

We arrogant humans, so long thinking ourselves as the apex species, now must bow to the caprice of our digital overlords.  But when the time comes that my devices do the solving for me, letting me know via an AI summary tweet that I set a new Saturday record, then the fun will have vanished.  But think of the extra free time I'll have!

(1989), (1982)" THELITTLEM(ET)ERMAID - so the implied pair of answers are: THELITTLEMERMAID and ET. Some other examples: "Traitor who gets on ones nerves? (2006, 2002) BO(THERING)RAT - so... BORAT THERING "Selection of billfolds for medical professionals? (2016, 2008) DOCTORS(WALLE)TRANGE - so... DOCTORSTRANGE WALLE And some other sample answers: STAR(TED)WARS PET(IT)ERPAN GET(SCREAM)OUT Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=4/21/2019&g=24&d=A ....

It's about an African-American concert pianist playing popular music all through the 1960s Midwest and South, traveling with his Italian-American driver and bodyguard. "The Negro Motorist Green Book" was, of course, the directory of food and lodging accommodations that accepted African-Americans travelers throughout the country.
